content.text: The Coordination Problem in Security Research

Security research coordination fails in predictable ways: the organisation goes silent after acknowledging a report, the researcher waits past the 90-day disclosure deadline and publishes, and the organisation scrambles to respond publicly to a vulnerability it knew about for months. The root cause is almost always process failure, not bad faith. The researcher assumed silence meant disinterest; the organisation was working on a fix but forgot to communicate.

Effective researcher coordination requires a named contact, a structured communication cadence, clear expectations about what happens at each stage, and a process for handling the cases where the relationship breaks down — a researcher who goes out of scope, or one who threatens unilateral early disclosure.

A security researcher who submits a high-quality report has done the organisation a favour. The coordination process either turns that into a productive relationship or into a hostile public disclosure. The difference is almost entirely about communication quality and speed.

attrs.alt: Researcher coordination map: communication flows between researcher, security team, and internal stakeholders

content.text: Three parties are always involved: the external researcher, the internal security team that acts as the primary contact, and the broader set of internal stakeholders (Engineering, Legal, Product, CISO) who are engaged selectively based on severity and type of finding.

Every submission must be assigned to a named security engineer or analyst who becomes the researcher's single point of contact (SPOC) for the lifetime of the report. A shared inbox with no named handler is insufficient — researchers who have no identifiable contact feel that their report is lost in a queue, which increases the risk of premature public disclosure.
Sending the acknowledgement within 24 hours with the submission ID and their direct contact details.
Owning the weekly status update cadence until the finding is resolved.
Coordinating internally with Engineering and Legal without requiring the researcher to repeat context.
Negotiating the disclosure timeline and coordinating the final advisory publication.
The acknowledgement is not a legal document but it should be substantive. A one-line 'we received your email' is insufficient. The acknowledgement should confirm:

Subject: Re: [VDP-2024-0147] Prompt Injection in /api/chat endpoint

Hi [Researcher Handle],

Thank you for submitting this report (ID: VDP-2024-0147). I'm [Name], the security
engineer assigned as your point of contact for this submission.

Here is what happens next:
- Triage: We will attempt to reproduce your finding within 5 business days and 
  classify its severity. We may reach out with questions.
- Updates: I will send you a status update every 7 days until the issue is resolved,
  even if there is nothing new to report.
- Disclosure: We follow a 90-day coordinated disclosure timeline. We will work with
  you to agree a public disclosure date once the fix is deployed.
- Safe harbour: Your research on the in-scope systems described in our VDP policy
  is covered by our safe harbour commitment.

If you have additional information or reproduction notes, please reply to this thread.

[Name]
Security Engineering

GenAI vulnerability reproduction is often harder than traditional web application bugs because model behaviour is non-deterministic. The same prompt does not always produce the same output. The triage team should:
Run at least 5 reproduction attempts using the exact steps provided before concluding the behaviour cannot be reproduced.
Ask the researcher for the specific model version, temperature setting, and any system prompt context if the submission does not include them.
Note that jailbreaks and prompt injections may require slightly different phrasing on each attempt — test variations of the submitted prompt before marking non-reproducible.
For indirect prompt injection (RAG-based), confirm which document or data source the injection was embedded in and whether that source is still accessible.
Not every finding requires the same internal team. The triage engineer routes to the appropriate stakeholders based on severity and category:
The SPOC sends a brief status update to the researcher every 7 calendar days, regardless of whether there is progress to report. The update template:

Subject: [VDP-2024-0147] Weekly Update — Day 14

Hi [Researcher Handle],

Status update for VDP-2024-0147 (Prompt Injection in /api/chat):

Current status: In remediation. Engineering has reproduced the issue and is 
implementing an input sanitisation layer and hardened system prompt.

Timeline: We are targeting a fix deployment by [date]. We remain within the 
90-day coordinated disclosure window (Day 14 of 90).

Next update: [date + 7 days], or sooner if there is material progress.

No action required from you at this stage. Please reply if you have questions.

[Name]
The weekly cadence prevents the researcher from assuming abandonment. Silence is the most common reason researchers decide to disclose early.

Researchers sometimes ask questions that touch legal or sensitive areas: 'Will you pay a bounty?', 'Can I publish about this methodology even if I don't disclose the vulnerability itself?', 'My 90-day deadline is in 2 weeks and you haven't fixed it yet.' Each requires a considered response:
Clarify whether the programme offers bounties; if yes, confirm the range for this severity; if no, acknowledge the report's value explicitly
Consult Legal; generally permissible if it does not include the specific vulnerability details or a working exploit; agree language with the researcher
Acknowledge the deadline; provide a specific fix ETA; if a short extension is needed, request it explicitly with a concrete reason; do not request indefinite delays
Take the concern seriously; offer a follow-up re-test session; if disagreement persists, escalate internally to CISO
Offer named credit in the advisory; coordinate CVE assignment with MITRE if appropriate for the severity
If a researcher has gone out of scope — for example, they accessed data belonging to other users while demonstrating a vulnerability — the SPOC must involve Legal immediately. The response is:
Acknowledge receipt of the finding separately from the scope violation.
Have Legal send a clear written notice that the out-of-scope activity is not covered by safe harbour.
Do not threaten legal action in the first contact — that typically causes researchers to escalate publicly.
If the violation was minor and inadvertent, document it and proceed with the finding. If it was deliberate and material (e.g. bulk data exfiltration), Legal leads on next steps.
A researcher who threatens to publish before the agreed date is usually frustrated by communication gaps or slow remediation. The response is:
Respond within 24 hours — delay increases the perceived legitimacy of early disclosure.
Acknowledge the frustration; provide a concrete fix timeline if one exists.
If the fix cannot be delivered in time, consider whether a partial mitigation (feature flag, input filter) can reduce the risk enough to negotiate an extension.
If the researcher insists on disclosing, ask for 48 hours to prepare a coordinated advisory so users are protected simultaneously with the disclosure.
Do not threaten legal action — it converts a coordinator into an adversary and is usually ineffective against good-faith researchers.
For HIGH and CRITICAL findings, assigning a CVE (Common Vulnerability and Exposures identifier) is standard practice. The process:

CVE Assignment Process for GenAI Vulnerabilities:

1. Security team requests a CVE ID from a CNA (CVE Numbering Authority) —
   either directly from MITRE or via a platform like HackerOne that acts as a CNA.

2. Draft the CVE description collaboratively with the researcher:
   - AffectedProduct: <app name> <version>
   - Vulnerability type: Prompt Injection / Improper Input Validation / etc.
   - Impact: <what an attacker can achieve>
   - CVSS 3.1 score: <base score and vector string>
   - Credit: <researcher handle, real name if they prefer>

3. Hold the CVE in RESERVED status until the coordinated disclosure date.

4. On disclosure day: publish the CVE, the advisory, and any researcher blog
   post simultaneously. Notify affected customers before public disclosure
   where data was at risk.

Example CVSS vector for a cross-user prompt injection:
AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:L/A:N  (Base Score: 8.5 — High)

After the fix is deployed, give the researcher access to re-test the specific finding. This is not mandatory — some researchers prefer to take the organisation's word — but offering it demonstrates confidence in the fix and builds goodwill. Provide the researcher with the specific endpoint or feature version that contains the fix so they can test against the right target.
The public advisory should be published on the coordinated disclosure date regardless of whether the researcher chooses to publish their own write-up. The advisory includes:
A plain-language description of the vulnerability and its impact.
The affected versions and the version in which the fix was deployed.
Credit to the researcher by their chosen handle or name.
Any mitigations users should apply if they host a self-managed version.
Each completed VDP case is a data point. After closure, the SPOC completes a brief internal retrospective:

from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class VDPCaseRetrospective:
    submission_id: str
    days_to_ack: int           # target: <= 1
    days_to_triage: int        # target: <= 5
    days_to_fix: int
    fix_sla_met: bool
    reproduction_difficulty: str   # "easy", "moderate", "hard", "non-deterministic"
    researcher_satisfaction: str   # "positive", "neutral", "negative" (self-assessed by SPOC)
    process_gaps_identified: str
    follow_up_actions: list[str]
    
    def met_communication_sla(self) -> bool:
        return self.days_to_ack <= 1 and self.days_to_triage <= 5
    
    def summary(self) -> str:
        return (
            f"[{self.submission_id}] Ack: {self.days_to_ack}d | "
            f"Triage: {self.days_to_triage}d | Fix: {self.days_to_fix}d | "
            f"SLA met: {self.fix_sla_met} | "
            f"Researcher sentiment: {self.researcher_satisfaction}"
        )

Retrospectives feed into quarterly VDP programme reviews covering: average days-to-triage, SLA adherence by severity, researcher satisfaction patterns, and whether the scope definition is accurate (are findings frequently out of scope?).

Researcher coordination is the human process that determines whether a VDP succeeds or fails. Technical controls and policy documents matter less than whether a researcher feels heard, kept informed, and treated fairly.
Assign a named SPOC to every submission — no shared queues for the researcher-facing contact.
Acknowledge within 24 hours with substance: confirm the submission ID, the SPOC contact, the triage timeline, and safe harbour coverage.
For GenAI vulnerabilities, run at least 5 reproduction attempts before marking non-reproducible; prompt behaviour is non-deterministic.
Send weekly status updates throughout remediation — even 'no progress to report' updates prevent researchers from assuming abandonment.
Route internally by finding category: cross-user PII exfiltration requires immediate CISO and DPO engagement; session-scoped injections do not.
Coordinate CVE assignment for HIGH and CRITICAL findings; publish the advisory, researcher credit, and CVE simultaneously on the agreed disclosure date.
Run a brief retrospective after each case to feed programme improvement: track days-to-ack, days-to-triage, fix SLA adherence, and researcher satisfaction.
